summary: DHH reflects on the inevitability of haters emerging whenever a project achieves real success, prompted by the growing backlash against his new Omarchy project. He recalls experiencing the same phenomenon twenty years ago with Ruby on Rails, when he naively believed logical arguments could convert critics. Citing Kathy Sierra's framework of love-vs-hate as opposing forces, he argues that passionate supporters and detractors are inseparable — you can't have one without the other. He concludes that haters should be seen as a milestone worth celebrating, since their presence signals you've created something that actually matters.
key_points: Success inevitably generates both passionate fans and passionate detractors — they are inseparable forces
Trying to logically argue with haters is naive and futile; DHH learned this lesson over twenty years
The safe middle ground where nobody hates you is also where nobody cares about your work
Not all criticism comes from haters, but once sufficient success is achieved, a large portion will
The arrival of haters should be celebrated as a milestone proving your creation matters
money_quotes: Nothing of note in this world is allowed to succeed without spawning a counteracting force of haters.

Back then I still thought you could argue your way to understanding. That if you just made a logical case to counter whatever objections were raised, you'd be able to persuade most haters to change their perspective. How naive.
If you want people to love your creation, you have to accept the opposing force. Yin and yang.
It's safe there in the gray middle. Nobody is mad at you, nobody is making any bad-faith arguments, but also, nobody cares.
When escape velocity is achieved, you can't avoid drawing energy from both sides.
Have you even made something worth cheering for, if there isn't a contingent there to boo at it too? Probably not.
So embrace the boos as you embrace the cheers. They come as a pair.
key_insight: Haters aren't a problem to solve but a signal to celebrate — their emergence proves your work has escaped the gray zone of irrelevance.
